commit 181442f7c83ab633a96d485aa2b0b649962cecaf
Author: Josh Law <objecting@objecting.org>

mm/damon/sysfs: check contexts->nr in repeat_call_fn

This commit fixes a NULL pointer dereference in damon_sysfs_repeat_call_fn().
It adds a check for contexts->nr to ensure contexts_arr[0] is not dereferenced
when nr_contexts is set to 0 via sysfs.

> diff --git a/mm/damon/sysfs.c b/mm/damon/sysfs.c
> index ddc30586c0e6..6a44a2f3d8fc 100644
> --- a/mm/damon/sysfs.c
> +++ b/mm/damon/sysfs.c
> @@ -1620,9 +1620,12 @@ static int damon_sysfs_repeat_call_fn(void *data)
>  
>  	if (!mutex_trylock(&damon_sysfs_lock))
>  		return 0;

While reviewing this function, I noticed that earlier in
damon_sysfs_repeat_call_fn(), a static global variable is used for timing:

static unsigned long damon_sysfs_next_update_jiffies;

static int damon_sysfs_repeat_call_fn(void *data)
{
        ...
        if (time_before(jiffies, damon_sysfs_next_update_jiffies))
                return 0;
        damon_sysfs_next_update_jiffies = jiffies +
                msecs_to_jiffies(sysfs_kdamond->refresh_ms);
        ...
}

Since damon_sysfs_next_update_jiffies is a static global variable, could this
cause update starvation across multiple kdamonds?

If multiple kdamond instances are running concurrently, they all read and
write this same global variable without synchronization. If Kdamond A has a
long refresh_ms and Kdamond B has a short refresh_ms, Kdamond A running first
will update the global timer to a later time.

When Kdamond B runs shortly after, time_before() would evaluate to true,
causing Kdamond B to return early without updating its stats.

> +	if (sysfs_kdamond->contexts->nr != 1)
> +		goto out;
>  	damon_sysfs_upd_tuned_intervals(sysfs_kdamond);
>  	damon_sysfs_upd_schemes_stats(sysfs_kdamond);
>  	damon_sysfs_upd_schemes_effective_quotas(sysfs_kdamond);
> +out:
>  	mutex_unlock(&damon_sysfs_lock);
>  	return 0;
>  }

Looking further into the surrounding sysfs code, could this leak the
repeat_call_control object if damon_call() fails?

In damon_sysfs_turn_damon_on(), we allocate the control and set
dealloc_on_cancel to true:

static int damon_sysfs_turn_damon_on(struct damon_sysfs_kdamond *kdamond)
{
        ...
        repeat_call_control = kmalloc_obj(*repeat_call_control);
        ...
        repeat_call_control->dealloc_on_cancel = true;
        damon_call(ctx, repeat_call_control);
        return err;
}

If the background kthread exits rapidly, damon_is_running() inside
damon_call() returns false. It then delegates to
damon_call_handle_inactive_ctx(), which deletes the control from the list and
returns -EINVAL without freeing the control. The dealloc_on_cancel logic is
only evaluated by the kthread.

Back in damon_sysfs_turn_damon_on(), the return value of damon_call() is
ignored, and the function returns the previous success err (0). The
dynamically allocated repeat_call_control is never freed.

Additionally, is it possible for damon_call() to deadlock and wait infinitely
if the background kdamond thread exits concurrently?

When damon_call() executes:

1. It acquires ctx->call_controls_lock, adds the control request, and releases
   the lock.
2. It checks damon_is_running() by acquiring kdamond_lock and verifying if
   ctx->kdamond is not NULL.

Concurrently, the background kdamond_fn() thread might be exiting. It invokes
kdamond_call(ctx, true) to drain pending controls, then acquires kdamond_lock,
sets ctx->kdamond to NULL, and exits.

If damon_call() inserts the new control into the list after kdamond_call()
has finished executing, but before ctx->kdamond is set to NULL,
damon_is_running() will still return true.

damon_call() will then proceed to wait_for_completion(&control->completion).
Because the kdamond thread has already finished processing the list and is
exiting, no one will ever complete the request, leaving the calling process
hung indefinitely in an uninterruptible sleep state.

Target Commit:
commit 181442f7c83ab633a96d485aa2b0b649962cecaf
Author: Josh Law <objecting@objecting.org>
Date:   Fri Mar 20 16:35:59 2026 +0000

    mm/damon/sysfs: check contexts->nr in repeat_call_fn
    
    damon_sysfs_repeat_call_fn() calls damon_sysfs_upd_tuned_intervals(),
    damon_sysfs_upd_schemes_stats(), and
    damon_sysfs_upd_schemes_effective_quotas() without checking
    contexts->nr.  If nr_contexts is set to 0 via sysfs while DAMON is
    running, these functions dereference contexts_arr[0] and cause a NULL
    pointer dereference.  Add the missing check.
    
    Fixes: d809a7c64ba8 ("mm/damon/sysfs: implement refresh_ms file internal work")
    Cc: <stable@vger.kernel.org> # 6.17.x
    Signed-off-by: Josh Law <objecting@objecting.org>
    Reviewed-by: SeongJae Park <sj@kernel.org>

diff --git a/mm/damon/sysfs.c b/mm/damon/sysfs.c
index ddc30586c0e6..6a44a2f3d8fc 100644
--- a/mm/damon/sysfs.c
+++ b/mm/damon/sysfs.c
@@ -1620,9 +1620,12 @@ static int damon_sysfs_repeat_call_fn(void *data)
 
 	if (!mutex_trylock(&damon_sysfs_lock))
 		return 0;
+	if (sysfs_kdamond->contexts->nr != 1)
+		goto out;
 	damon_sysfs_upd_tuned_intervals(sysfs_kdamond);
 	damon_sysfs_upd_schemes_stats(sysfs_kdamond);
 	damon_sysfs_upd_schemes_effective_quotas(sysfs_kdamond);
+out:
 	mutex_unlock(&damon_sysfs_lock);
 	return 0;
 }

Now the concern surfaces - if `state_store()` is holding the `damon_sysfs_lock` while also waiting for the completion of the worker thread... that seems like a deadlock potential.

    "thought": "**Analyzing a Race Condition in DAMON Sysfs Interaction**

Okay, let's break this down. My initial focus was on `damon_sysfs_schemes_tried_regions_upd_one()`, specifically the fact that it's a callback within the `kdamond` thread. I immediately thought about potential blocking behavior of `damos_walk()` called by `damon_sysfs_update_schemes_tried_regions()`. The crucial detail is that `damos_walk()` *does* block, using `wait_for_completion()`. And crucially, it holds `damon_sysfs_lock` while blocking.

This is where the race condition starts to materialize. If `state_store` holds the lock, calls `damos_walk()`, and blocks while waiting for the `kdamond` thread to finish the walk, the lock is held for an extended period. During this entire walk, `damon_sysfs_lock` is held. So, another process cannot change the number of contexts, `contexts->nr`, since that requires `damon_sysfs_lock`.

Initially, I considered whether `contexts->nr` could be changed *before* `damos_walk()` is called. However, `damon_sysfs_handle_cmd()` performs a check: `kdamond->contexts->nr != 1`. This check is in place *before* even calling the function which calls `damos_walk()`. If `nr` is not 1, the command is rejected immediately with `-EINVAL`, and `damos_walk()` is never entered.

The plot thickens with `damon_sysfs_repeat_call_fn()`. When DAMON is enabled, `damon_sysfs_turn_damon_on()` sets up a repeating call using `damon_call()`, which calls `damon_sysfs_repeat_call_fn()` periodically within `kdamond`. The lock is taken using `mutex_trylock()`. Previously, this function did *not* check `sysfs_kdamond->contexts->nr`. Therefore, if someone were to modify `contexts->nr` to 0 (which would require the lock) and then the `kdamond` thread awoke, it would encounter this check! Since it previously did not exist, the code would dereference `contexts_arr[0]` without ensuring that context `0` actually existed! So the patch fixes this precisely.

Finally, I considered other places where the `contexts_arr[0]` might be dereferenced without checking the number of contexts. Specifically, the functions called from `damon_sysfs_repeat_call_fn()`: `damon_sysfs_upd_schemes_stats`, `damon_sysfs_upd_schemes_effective_quotas`, and `damon_sysfs_upd_tuned_intervals`. These functions themselves don't check `nr`, but `damon_sysfs_repeat_call_fn()` does *now* check `nr` before calling them, which prevents any further issues.

The last part of the analysis concerns `damon_sysfs_damon_call()`, which is used by `damon_sysfs_handle_cmd()` for various commands like updating scheme stats. I investigated whether the functions called through this mechanism (e.g., `damon_sysfs_upd_schemes_stats`) performed a check on `nr`? Specifically, if the command requires that `nr` is 1, `damon_sysfs_handle_cmd()` will check that this is true before calling `damon_sysfs_damon_call()`. This is also safe since this function then schedules the work on `kdamond`.

    "thought": "**Vulnerability Analysis of DAMON's `nr_contexts` Handling**

Okay, here's what I've got. I'm focusing on how `nr_contexts` is handled in DAMON's sysfs interface.  It seems there's a serious potential vulnerability here.

First, I noticed that `nr_contexts_store`, which handles writes to the `nr_contexts` sysfs file, takes the `damon_sysfs_lock`... but it *doesn't* check if the kdamond (the monitoring thread) is running via a call to `damon_sysfs_kdamond_running()`!  This is a big red flag. It means I can potentially write `0` to `nr_contexts` even while the kdamond is actively monitoring.

Now, if I do that, the `contexts->nr` field in the `struct damon_sysfs_contexts` becomes 0, and crucially, `contexts->contexts_arr` gets freed and set to `NULL` via `damon_sysfs_contexts_rm_dirs`.

The code doesn't even attempt to find the `kdamond` structure at that point, which means it isn't even checking if the thread is available.

The concern is: what happens *after* `contexts->contexts_arr` is kfree'd? `damon_sysfs_contexts_add_dirs` is called by writing a new value for `nr_contexts`.  This can lead to a race condition. It looks like `damon_sysfs_contexts_rm_dirs` will loop through `contexts->nr` entries and remove the sysfs directories, effectively deleting them. If `nr_contexts` is set to `0`, `damon_sysfs_repeat_call_fn` gets called later because the timer `refresh_ms` was set to refresh. This timer can call a function `damon_sysfs_repeat_call_fn`. The `damon_sysfs_repeat_call_fn` will lock `damon_sysfs_lock`. This serialize the `damon_sysfs_lock`. If `nr` is 0 at this time, it gets skipped by the `contexts->nr != 1` check. So this is what the patch fixes!

But then I started thinking about the sysfs commands.  If I try to send a command like `update_tuned_intervals` to the `state` sysfs file, the `damon_sysfs_handle_cmd` function is called.  This function checks `kdamond->contexts->nr != 1`.  So if `nr_contexts` is 0 at this time, `damon_sysfs_handle_cmd` returns `-EINVAL`. This protects the explicit sysfs commands.

Here's the problem: even if I set `nr_contexts` to 0, the kdamond's monitoring thread continues to run. It does NOT stop the underlying DAMON context, `kdamond->damon_ctx`!  The `damon_sysfs_repeat_call_fn` is a callback registered in `damon_ctx`.

What's really scary is that if I subsequently try to set `nr_contexts` back to 1, I'm allocating a new context. But the `kdamond->damon_ctx` still points to the *old*, potentially invalid context. `damon_sysfs_contexts_rm_dirs` drops `kobject_put` on the context. But it does NOT free `kdamond->damon_ctx`. The core DAMON subsystem is still running.

So, setting `nr_contexts` to 0 is like putting the sysfs interface into an inconsistent state. The core DAMON functionality continues, but the sysfs view becomes detached. This is a critical race condition.

    "thought": "**Analyzing a Potential NULL Pointer Dereference in DAMON's sysfs Interface**

Alright, let's break this down. I'm looking at how the DAMON (Data Access MONitor) sysfs interface handles contexts within its `kdamond` structure. Specifically, I'm focusing on a potential vulnerability when the number of contexts (`nr`) is changed dynamically via sysfs while DAMON is active.

First, I see that the `damon_sysfs_kdamond_add_dirs` function is responsible for allocating the `contexts` struct, which contains an array, `contexts_arr`, where the DAMON contexts are stored. If this allocation fails, `kdamond->contexts` might not be allocated. However, if the allocation *succeeds*, the `contexts` struct is correctly allocated.

The critical area is the `damon_sysfs_repeat_call_fn` function. This function calls several update functions: `damon_sysfs_upd_tuned_intervals`, `damon_sysfs_upd_schemes_stats`, and `damon_sysfs_upd_schemes_effective_quotas`. I see a new `if` check added: `if (sysfs_kdamond->contexts->nr != 1)`. The original code did not contain this check. It seems that this new check prevents execution of those update functions.

Within `damon_sysfs_upd_tuned_intervals`, it dereferences `kdamond->contexts->contexts_arr[0]`. If `contexts->nr` is not 1, this means that the array is either empty or potentially contains some other values. The patch addresses the case where `nr` becomes 0. A condition of `nr = 0` causes a potential null pointer dereference, or out-of-bounds array access in `contexts_arr[0]`, leading to a crash.

My next thought is, is it possible for `nr` to be *greater* than 1? Looking at the `damon_sysfs_turn_damon_on` function, I notice this check: `if (kdamond->contexts->nr != 1) return -EINVAL;`. So, DAMON is explicitly limited to supporting only *one* context per `kdamond` currently, at least for the "on" state. Thus, an `nr > 1` scenario should be prevented at the point DAMON turns on.

Okay, so the vulnerability arises if a user, while DAMON is *running*, writes 0 to the `nr_contexts` sysfs entry. This would change `nr` to 0. That operation could cause a free, followed by dereferencing the freed memory. The patch adds the safety check to prevent dereferencing `contexts_arr[0]` when `nr` is not 1.

Now, let's investigate the `nr_contexts_store` function to see exactly how this value is being updated. I need to understand what writes to `nr_contexts` do.

    "thought": "**Analyzing a Potential Data Race in DAMON's Sysfs Interface**

Okay, so here's the situation I'm looking at. We've got a scenario where `damon_sysfs_contexts_add_dirs` is called, and this function then calls `damon_sysfs_contexts_rm_dirs`. The critical part is that `damon_sysfs_contexts_rm_dirs` frees the `contexts_arr` and subsequently sets `contexts->nr` to zero. If `nr_contexts` ends up being written as zero, then `contexts->nr` becomes zero, and `contexts->contexts_arr` gets set to NULL.

Now, the potential race condition arises because `damon_sysfs_repeat_call_fn` gets called repeatedly via a timer or workqueue, which calls `damon_call`.  This function locks `damon_sysfs_lock`.  The problem is, `nr_contexts_store`, which *also* uses `damon_sysfs_lock`. This means `damon_sysfs_repeat_call_fn` and `nr_contexts_store` are mutually exclusive over that lock.

Here's the sequence.  `nr_contexts_store` can write zero, which then calls `damon_sysfs_contexts_add_dirs(contexts, 0)`. That, in turn, calls `damon_sysfs_contexts_rm_dirs(contexts)`. This frees `contexts->contexts_arr` and sets `contexts->nr = 0`.  Then `nr_contexts_store` releases `damon_sysfs_lock`.

After the lock is released, `damon_sysfs_repeat_call_fn` takes the lock.  Before the patch, `damon_sysfs_repeat_call_fn` would have called `damon_sysfs_upd_tuned_intervals`. The function accesses `contexts->contexts_arr[0]`. Since the array might be NULL or empty at that point, this dereferences NULL.

The patch correctly adds a check: `if (sysfs_kdamond->contexts->nr != 1) goto out;` which prevents the dereference.

My initial question is whether the patch itself has issues. Then, I needed to know if any other code paths access `contexts->contexts_arr[0]` without also checking `contexts->nr`. Let's dig in.

I went through the code to identify other potential areas.

*   `damon_sysfs_turn_damon_on`, `damon_sysfs_commit_input`, and `damon_sysfs_commit_schemes_quota_goals` all check if `contexts->nr` is equal to 1. This prevents any issues.

*   `damon_sysfs_upd_schemes_effective_quotas` calls `damos_sysfs_update_effective_quotas` which takes `contexts->contexts_arr[0]`.

*   `damon_sysfs_handle_cmd`, is the command dispatcher, and it checks for `nr != 1` before invoking any command, EXCEPT `DAMON_SYSFS_CMD_OFF`. `DAMON_SYSFS_CMD_UPDATE_SCHEMES_EFFECTIVE_QUOTAS`, `DAMON_SYSFS_CMD_UPDATE_TUNED_INTERVALS`, and `DAMON_SYSFS_CMD_UPDATE_SCHEMES_STATS` are handled here, so are safe.

But, `damon_sysfs_repeat_call_fn` is NOT dispatched from sysfs command! It's dispatched via `damon_call` callback. And it calls the functions, after taking the lock:
    -   `damon_sysfs_upd_tuned_intervals`
    -   `damon_sysfs_upd_schemes_stats`
    -   `damon_sysfs_upd_schemes_effective_quotas`

With the patch, `damon_sysfs_repeat_call_fn` will check `nr != 1` before calling those three.

However, I need to know: Are there any other functions called via `damon_call` or `damos_walk` that might access `contexts_arr[0]`? That's the critical question to consider.

Okay, so I've been digging into this DAMON sysfs code, specifically focusing on how context counts (`nr`) are handled and whether we're vulnerable to null pointer dereferences. It looks like the issue stems from a potential race condition when the number of contexts is modified, specifically going from 1 to 0.

The core of the problem seems to be in `damon_sysfs_repeat_call_fn`. This function is called periodically, likely by a timer or kdamond loop. The critical section is guarded by `damon_sysfs_lock` to avoid races, but the context count can be changed while this function executes. The fix provided checks `sysfs_kdamond->contexts->nr != 1` and skips the update if it's not the case.

My initial thoughts are that even if `sysfs_schemes->nr` changes, the `damon_sysfs_schemes_update_stats` function seems to handle it gracefully with a check for `schemes_idx >= sysfs_schemes->nr` using `break`. Similarly, calling `damon_sysfs_upd_tuned_intervals` *should* be fine if `contexts_arr[0]` is newly created, as `damon_sysfs_context_set_attrs` and `damon_sysfs_attrs_add_dirs` are responsible for initializing `attrs->intervals`.

However, the major concern is when `nr_contexts` drops to 0. In this state, `contexts_arr[0]` becomes a NULL pointer (or invalid memory). The patch fixes this by skipping updates when `nr != 1`. I then started to wonder if *any* other code path was missing that check.

I examined `damon_sysfs_damon_call` to check where that code is executed. It turns out that `damon_sysfs_handle_cmd` checks `nr != 1` before processing any commands (except `DAMON_SYSFS_CMD_OFF`). This seems safe. The *only* other place where these updates are triggered is asynchronously by the timer loop in `damon_sysfs_repeat_call_fn`, which is *exactly* what the patch addresses.

I also double-checked `state_show` and `pid_show` and those don't access `contexts` at all. Furthermore, I went looking in `mm/damon/sysfs.c` around line 1523 and all the functions that access `contexts->contexts_arr[0]` seem to have the `nr != 1` check.

Looking back at `damon_sysfs_repeat_call_fn`, the patch seems correct: if `sysfs_kdamond->contexts->nr != 1`, it jumps to `out` after releasing the mutex. This is the fix, and I think it is sound.

    "thought": "**Comprehensive Analysis of DAMON Sysfs Concurrency and Safety**

Okay, so the initial concern was whether removing targets while DAMON is running could lead to problems, specifically concerning the `targets_arr` being freed while in use. Let's break this down. First, the `damon_sysfs_targets_add_dirs` function does indeed free `targets_arr`. However, the critical part is that when DAMON is *running*, `damon_sysfs_repeat_call_fn` *doesn't* actually touch `targets_arr`. This function is the central piece of this analysis. I needed to ensure that *nothing* accesses that array while DAMON is active, and it is. The `damon_sysfs_commit_input` and `damon_sysfs_turn_damon_on` functions do use the array, but they're executed by the sysfs thread, which holds `damon_sysfs_lock`. This is good, mutually exclusive access.

The `nr_targets_store` function also needs this lock. Therefore, they are mutually exclusive. I then explored `damos_walk` in `damon_sysfs_update_schemes_tried_regions`, which is called from `damon_sysfs_handle_cmd` (while holding the lock), and the callback `damon_sysfs_schemes_tried_regions_upd_one` accesses `sysfs_kdamond->contexts->contexts_arr[0]->schemes`. Again, the lock ensures that `nr_targets_store` and `nr_schemes_store` cannot run concurrently. Thus, everything is safe from concurrency EXCEPT `damon_sysfs_repeat_call_fn`.

The key vulnerability lies in `damon_sysfs_repeat_call_fn`, because it is called by the kdamond thread (using `mutex_trylock` instead of being directly triggered by a sysfs command, which would have guaranteed the lock is held). Since it's using `mutex_trylock`, this could succeed if the sysfs thread drops the lock. The core fix appears to be that the patch correctly skips updates in `repeat_call_fn` by checking `nr != 1`.

   Single allocations:     kmalloc(sizeof(TYPE), ...)\n    are replaced with:      kmalloc_obj(TYPE, ...)\n    \n    Array allocations:      kmalloc_array(COUNT, sizeof(TYPE), ...)\n    are replaced This means memory is leaked for\n    every successful online DAMON parameters commit.  Fix the leak by always\n    deallocating it.\n    \n    Link: https://lkml.kernel.org/r/20251003201455.41448-3-sj@kernel.org\n    Fixes: 4c9ea539ad59 (\"mm/damon/sysfs: validate user inputs from damon_sysfs_commit_input()\")\n    Signed-off-by: SeongJae Park <sj@kernel.org>\n    Cc: <stable@vger.kernel.org>    [6.15+]\n    Signed-off-by: Andrew Morton <akpm@linux-foundation.org>\n\ndiff --git a/mm/damon/sysfs.c b/mm/damon/sysfs.c\nindex 703f55a91b3c..cd6815ecc04e 100644\n--- a/mm/damon/sysfs.c\n+++ b/mm/damon/sysfs.c\n@@ -1476,12 +1476,11 @@ static int damon_sysfs_commit_input(void *data)\n \tif (!test_ctx)\n \t\treturn -ENOMEM;\n \terr = damon_commit_ctx(test_ctx, param_ctx);\n-\tif (err) {\n-\t\tdamon_destroy_ctx(test_ctx);\n+\tif (err)\n \t\tgoto out;\n-\t}\n \terr = damon_commit_ctx(kdamond->damon_ctx, param_ctx);\n out:\n+\tdamon_destroy_ctx(test_ctx);\n \tdamon_destroy_ctx(param_ctx);\n \treturn err;\n }\n\ncommit f0c5118ebb0eb7e4fd6f0d2ace3315ca141b317f\nAuthor: SeongJae Park <sj@kernel.org>\nDate:   Fri Oct 3 13:14:54 2025 -0700\n\n    mm/damon/sysfs: catch commit test ctx alloc failure\n    \n    Patch series \"mm/damon/sysfs: fix commit test damon_ctx [de]allocation\".\n    \n    DAMON sysfs interface dynamically allocates and uses a damon_ctx object\n    for testing if given inputs for online DAMON parameters update is valid.\n    The object is being used without an allocation failure check, and leaked\n    when the test succeeds.  Fix the two bugs.\n    \n    \n    This patch (of 2):\n    \n    The damon_ctx for testing online DAMON parameters commit inputs is used\n    without its allocation failure check.  This could result in an invalid\n    memory access.  Fix it by directly returning an error when the allocation\n    failed.\n    \n    Link: https://lkml.kernel.org/r/20251003201455.41448-1-sj@kernel.org\n    Link: https://lkml.kernel.org/r/20251003201455.41448-2-sj@kernel.org\n    Fixes: 4c9ea539ad59 (\"mm/damon/sysfs: validate user inputs from damon_sysfs_commit_input()\")\n    Signed-off-by: SeongJae Park <sj@kernel.org>\n    Cc: <stable@vger.kernel.org>    [6.15+]\n    Signed-off-by: Andrew Morton <akpm@linux-foundation.org>\n\ndiff --git a/mm/damon/sysfs.c b/mm/damon/sysfs.c\nindex 2fc722f998f8..703f55a91b3c 100644\n--- a/mm/damon/sysfs.c\n+++ b/mm/damon/sysfs.c\n@@ -1473,6 +1473,8 @@ static int damon_sysfs_commit_input(void *data)\n \tif (IS_ERR(param_ctx))\n \t\treturn PTR_ERR(param_ctx);\n \ttest_ctx = damon_new_ctx();\n+\tif (!test_ctx)\n+\t\treturn -ENOMEM;\n \terr = damon_commit_ctx(test_ctx, param_ctx);\n \tif (err) {\n \t\tdamon_destroy_ctx(test_ctx);\n\ncommit 8804d970fab45726b3c7cd7f240b31122aa94219\nMerge: 24d9e8b3c9c8 1367da7eb875\nAuthor: Linus Torvalds <torvalds@linux-foundation.org>\nDate:   Thu Oct 2 18:18:33 2025 -0700\n\n    Merge tag 'mm-stable-2025-10-01-19-00' of git://git.kernel.org/pub/scm/linux/kernel/git/akpm/mm\n    \n    Pull MM updates from Andrew Morton:\n    \n     - \"mm, swap: improve cluster scan strategy\" from Kairui Song improves\n       performance and reduces the failure rate of swap cluster allocation\n    \n     - \"support large align and nid in Rust allocators\" from Vitaly Wool\n       permits Rust allocators to set NUMA node and large alignment when\n       perforning slub and vmalloc reallocs\n    \n     - \"mm/damon/vaddr: support stat-purpose DAMOS\" from Yueyang Pan extend\n       DAMOS_STAT's handling of the DAMON operations sets for virtual\n       address spaces for ops-level DAMOS filters\n    \n     - \"execute PROCMAP_QUERY ioctl under per-vma lock\" from Suren\n       Baghdasaryan reduces mmap_lock contention during reads of\n       /proc/pid/maps\n    \n     - \"mm/mincore: minor clean up for swap cache checking\" from Kairui Song\n       performs some cleanup in the swap code\n    \n     - \"mm: vm_normal_page*() improvements\" from David Hildenbrand provides\n       code cleanup in the pagemap code\n    \n     - \"add persistent huge zero folio support\" from Pankaj Raghav provides\n       a block layer speedup by optionalls making the\n       huge_zero_pagepersistent, instead of releasing it when its refcount\n       falls to zero\n    \n     - \"kho: fixes and cleanups\" from Mike Rapoport adds a few touchups to\n       the recently added Kexec Handover feature\n    \n     - \"mm: make mm->flags a bitmap and 64-bit on all arches\" from Lorenzo\n       Stoakes turns mm_struct.flags into a bitmap. To end the constant\n       struggle with space shortage on 32-bit conflicting with 64-bit's\n       needs\n    \n     - \"mm/swapfile.c and swap.h cleanup\" from Chris Li cleans up some swap\n       code\n    \n     - \"selftests/mm: Fix false positives and skip unsupported tests\" from\n       Donet Tom fixes a few things in our selftests code\n    \n     - \"prctl: extend PR_SET_THP_DISABLE to only provide THPs when advised\"\n       from David Hildenbrand \"allows individual processes to opt-out of\n       THP=always into THP=madvise, without affecting other workloads on the\n       system\".\n    \n       It's a long story - the [1/N] changelog spells out the considerations\n    \n     - \"Add and use memdesc_flags_t\" from Matthew Wilcox gets us started on\n       the memdesc project. Please see\n    \n          https://kernelnewbies.org/MatthewWilcox/Memdescs and\n          https://blogs.oracle.com/linux/post/introducing-memdesc\n    \n     - \"Tiny optimization for large read operations\" from Chi Zhiling\n       improves the efficiency of the pagecache read path\n    \n     - \"Better split_huge_page_test result check\" from Zi Yan improves our\n       folio splitting selftest code\n    \n     - \"test that rmap behaves as expected\" from Wei Yang adds some rmap\n       selftests\n    \n     - \"remove write_cache_pages()\" from Christoph Hellwig removes that\n       function and converts its two remaining callers\n    \n     - \"selftests/mm: uffd-stress fixes\" from Dev Jain fixes some UFFD\n       selftests issues\n    \n     - \"introduce kernel file mapped folios\" from Boris Burkov introduces\n       the concept of \"kernel file pages\". Using these permits btrfs to\n       account its metadata pages to the root cgroup, rather than to the\n       cgroups of random inappropriate tasks\n    \n     - \"mm/pageblock: improve readability of some pageblock handling\" from\n       Wei Yang provides some readability improvements to the page allocator\n       code\n    \n     - \"mm/damon: support ARM32 with LPAE\" from SeongJae Park teaches DAMON\n       to understand arm32 highmem\n    \n     - \"tools: testing: Use existing atomic.h for vma/maple tests\" from\n       Brendan Jackman performs some code cleanups and deduplication under\n       tools/testing/\n    \n     - \"maple_tree: Fix testing for 32bit compiles\" from Liam Howlett fixes\n       a couple of 32-bit issues in tools/testing/radix-tree.c\n    \n     - \"kasan: unify kasan_enabled() and remove arch-specific\n       implementations\" from Sabyrzhan Tasbolatov moves KASAN arch-specific\n       initialization code into a common arch-neutral implementation\n    \n     - \"mm: remove zpool\" from Johannes Weiner removes zspool - an\n       indirection layer which now only redirects to a single thing\n       (zsmalloc)\n    \n     - \"mm: task_stack: Stack handling cleanups\" from Pasha Tatashin makes a\n       couple of cleanups in the fork code\n    \n     - \"mm: remove nth_page()\" from David Hildenbrand makes rather a lot of\n       adjustments at various nth_page() callsites, eventually permitting\n       the removal of that undesirable helper function\n    \n     - \"introduce kasan.write_only option in hw-tags\" from Yeoreum Yun\n       creates a KASAN read-only mode for ARM, using that architecture's\n       memory tagging feature. It is felt that a read-only mode KASAN is\n       suitable for use in production systems rather than debug-only\n    \n     - \"mm: hugetlb: cleanup hugetlb folio allocation\" from Kefeng Wang does\n       some tidying in the hugetlb folio allocation code\n    \n     - \"mm: establish const-correctness for pointer parameters\" from Max\n       Kellermann makes quite a number of the MM API functions more accurate\n       about the constness of their arguments. This was getting in the way\n       of subsystems (in this case CEPH) when they attempt to improving\n       their own const/non-const accuracy\n    \n     - \"Cleanup free_pages() misuse\" from Vishal Moola fixes a number of\n       code sites which were confused over when to use free_pages() vs\n       __free_pages()\n    \n     - \"Add Rust abstraction for Maple Trees\" from Alice Ryhl makes the\n       mapletree code accessible to Rust. Required by nouveau and by its\n       forthcoming successor: the new Rust Nova driver\n    \n     - \"selftests/mm: split_huge_page_test: split_pte_mapped_thp\n       improvements\" from David Hildenbrand adds a fix and some cleanups to\n       the thp selftesting code\n    \n     - \"mm, swap: introduce swap table as swap cache (phase I)\" from Chris\n       Li and Kairui Song is the first step along the path to implementing\n       \"swap tables\" - a new approach to swap allocation and state tracking\n       which is expected to yield speed and space improvements. This\n       patchset itself yields a 5-20% performance benefit in some situations\n    \n     - \"Some ptdesc cleanups\" from Matthew Wilcox utilizes the new memdesc\n       layer to clean up the ptdesc code a little\n    \n     - \"Fix va_high_addr_switch.sh test failure\" from Chunyu Hu fixes some\n       issues in our 5-level pagetable selftesting code\n    \n     - \"Minor fixes for memory allocation profiling\" from Suren Baghdasaryan\n       addresses a couple of minor issues in relatively new memory\n       allocation profiling feature\n    \n     - \"Small cleanups\" from Matthew Wilcox has a few cleanups in\n       preparation for more memdesc work\n    \n     - \"mm/damon: add addr_unit for DAMON_LRU_SORT and DAMON_RECLAIM\" from\n       Quanmin Yan makes some changes to DAMON in furtherance of supporting\n       arm highmem\n    \n     - \"selftests/mm: Add -Wunreachable-code and fix warnings\" from Muhammad\n       Anjum adds that compiler check to selftests code and fixes the\n       fallout, by removing dead code\n    \n     - \"Improvements to Victim Process Thawing and OOM Reaper Traversal\n       Order\" from zhongjinji makes a number of improvements in the OOM\n       killer: mainly thawing a more appropriate group of victim threads so\n       they can release resources\n    \n     - \"mm/damon: misc fixups and improvements for 6.18\" from SeongJae Park\n       is a bunch of small and unrelated fixups for DAMON\n    \n     - \"mm/damon: define and use DAMON initialization check function\" from\n       SeongJae Park implement reliability and maintainability improvements\n       to a recently-added bug fix\n    \n     - \"mm/damon/stat: expose auto-tuned intervals and non-idle ages\" from\n       SeongJae Park provides additional transparency to userspace clients\n       of the DAMON_STAT information\n    \n     - \"Expand scope of khugepaged anonymous collapse\" from Dev Jain removes\n       some constraints on khubepaged's collapsing of anon VMAs. It also\n       increases the success rate of MADV_COLLAPSE against an anon vma\n    \n     - \"mm: do not assume file == vma->vm_file in compat_vma_mmap_prepare()\"\n       from Lorenzo Stoakes moves us further towards removal of\n       file_operations.mmap(). ...\n\ncommit 06195ee967d06ead757f9291bbaf1a0b30fa10b8\nAuthor: Akinobu Mita <akinobu.mita@gmail.com>\nDate:   Sat Sep 20 22:25:46 2025 +0900\n\n    mm/damon/sysfs: do not ignore callback's return Fix it.\n    \n    Link: https://lkml.kernel.org/r/20250920132546.5822-1-akinobu.mita@gmail.com\n    Fixes: f64539dcdb87 (\"mm/damon/sysfs: use damon_call() for update_schemes_stats\")\n    Signed-off-by: Akinobu Mita <akinobu.mita@gmail.com>\n    Reviewed-by: SeongJae Park <sj@kernel.org>\n    Cc: <stable@vger.kernel.org>    [6.14+]\n    Signed-off-by: Andrew Morton <akpm@linux-foundation.org>\n\ndiff --git a/mm/damon/sysfs.c b/mm/damon/sysfs.c\nindex c96c2154128f..7308dee97b21 100644\n--- a/mm/damon/sysfs.c\n+++ b/mm/damon/sysfs.c\n@@ -1592,12 +1592,14 @@ static int damon_sysfs_damon_call(int (*fn)(void *data),\n \t\tstruct damon_sysfs_kdamond *kdamond)\n {\n \tstruct damon_call_control call_control = {};\n+\tint err;\n \n \tif (!kdamond->damon_ctx)\n \t\treturn -EINVAL;\n \tcall_control.fn = fn;\n \tcall_control.data = kdamond;\n-\treturn damon_call(kdamond->damon_ctx, &call_control);\n+\terr = damon_call(kdamond->damon_ctx, &call_control);\n+\treturn err ? err : call_control.return_code;\n }\n \n struct damon_sysfs_schemes_walk_data {\n\ncommit 8d009da32f13759ee7a6ec002ba62dc8faeb6423\nAuthor: SeongJae Park <sj@kernel.org>\nDate:   Wed Sep 17 08:31:54 2025 -0700\n\n    mm/damon/sysfs: set damon_ctx->min_sz_region only for paddr use case\n    \n    damon_ctx->addr_unit is respected only for physical address space\n    monitoring use case. Alright, let's break this down. As an expert in this field, I've identified a couple of potential issues in this DAMON sysfs code, with a focus on memory safety, concurrency, and asynchronous task management.

**Concern 1: Memory leak of `repeat_call_control`**

The first big problem is a classic memory leak in `damon_sysfs_turn_damon_on()`. If `damon_call()` fails for whatever reason – let's say the DAMON context terminates rapidly or the kthread exits before the call – we've got a `repeat_call_control` object that's allocated with `kmalloc_obj`, but never freed.

Here's the chain of events:

1.  `repeat_call_control` is allocated.
2.  `damon_call()` is used to schedule a function on a background thread.
3.  If `damon_call` fails (e.g., `-EINVAL` because the kthread exits), the `control` is removed from the list within the function itself, and an error is returned.
4.  The caller (`damon_sysfs_turn_damon_on`) does *not* check for a failure and simply returns whatever error code came from `damon_start`.  Crucially, it skips the `kfree(repeat_call_control)`.
5.  Because it's no longer on the list, the background thread won't be able to deallocate it (since the list is empty).  This means the allocated memory is lost.

This is a pretty straightforward memory leak, and it's easy to miss without careful attention to the return values of asynchronous functions.

**Concern 2: Global State / Starvation of background sysfs refresh tasks**

The second issue is a bit more subtle, but potentially more impactful in real-world scenarios. We've got this global variable, `damon_sysfs_next_update_jiffies`, that's used to control when the background sysfs refresh tasks should run. This is defined as `static unsigned long damon_sysfs_next_update_jiffies`.

1.  When a kdamond is "turned on," this global variable gets initialized based on *that* specific kdamond's `refresh_ms` (refresh interval) value.
2.  The background thread checks `time_before(jiffies, damon_sysfs_next_update_jiffies)`.
3.  If the timestamp hasn't passed, it returns early. Otherwise, it updates the global variable *again* with *its own* `refresh_ms`.
4.  If multiple kdamonds are running concurrently, they're all reading and writing *the same* global variable.

The main problem here is if we have two kdamonds with wildly different refresh intervals. For instance, if kdamond 0 has a long refresh time, and kdamond 1 has a very short refresh time, kdamond 1 will be starved for the entire duration of the long interval set by kdamond 0. This is a very real problem and likely to impact performance and responsiveness!

The fix is simple: This `damon_sysfs_next_update_jiffies` timer should be part of the `struct damon_sysfs_kdamond` structure.

**Concern 3: Deadlock / Infinite Wait**

There's a critical race condition that can lead to deadlock/infinite wait. `damon_sysfs_damon_call` calls `damon_call` and waits for completion. The background thread calls the completion. The problem occurs when the background thread is exiting. Here's a quick summary.

1.  Thread is exiting.
2.  Thread calls `damon_call(ctx, true)`.
3.  CPU switches to another thread.
4.  Another thread calls `damon_call(ctx, &control)`.
5.  `damon_is_running(ctx)` returns true because `ctx->kdamond` has not been set to null yet.
6.  `wait_for_completion(&control->completion)` is called. The thread blocks FOREVER!
7.  CPU switches back to kdamond thread. The thread exits, setting `ctx->kdamond = NULL;`.
8.  The other thread is blocked forever.

In essence, a thread can enter the blocked completion queue just as the kthread exits. Then it can block forever.

Okay, so I saw that `TODO.md` entry in my previous search, pointing to a use of `mutex_trylock` rather than `mutex_lock` for `damon_sysfs_lock`. That immediately grabbed my attention because it's an unusual pattern, and I always get suspicious of those.

Right, so `mutex_trylock` doesn't behave like a typical `trylock`. It returns 1 on success and 0 on failure, unlike a normal trylock. That's confirmed. Now, let's examine the code snippet to confirm. The code does check for failure and returns 0. So, it acquires the lock *only* if `mutex_trylock` returns 1, as expected. I confirm this quickly by mentally executing the logic.

Looking at the full context of the lock usage, the code seems fine, the lock is released in all cases.

But wait, the prompt asks me to consider issues like Use-After-Free, potential deadlocks, missed unlocks, and lifecycle problems of the mutex. I need to make sure I don't miss anything.

So I look at the error path:

```c
	if (!mutex_trylock(&damon_sysfs_lock))
		return 0;
	if (sysfs_kdamond->contexts->nr != 1)
		goto out;
```

If `sysfs_kdamond->contexts` is NULL, what happens? And if `sysfs_kdamond` can be modified concurrently.

The lock `damon_sysfs_lock` is taken. So `sysfs_kdamond` and `contexts` should be safe, assuming `contexts` is not NULL.

Could `contexts` be NULL?

I'm starting to get a nagging feeling about the potential for a NULL pointer dereference, specifically with that `sysfs_kdamond->contexts` access. I want to check how the struct `sysfs_kdamond` is initialized.

Looking at `damon_sysfs_repeat_call_fn`:

```c
static int damon_sysfs_repeat_call_fn(void *data)
{
	struct damon_sysfs_kdamond *sysfs_kdamond = data;

	if (!sysfs_kdamond->refresh_ms)
		return 0;
	if (time_before(jiffies, damon_sysfs_next_update_jiffies))
		return 0;
	damon_sysfs_next_update_jiffies = jiffies +
		msecs_to_jiffies(sysfs_kdamond->refresh_ms);

	if (!mutex_trylock(&damon_sysfs_lock))
		return 0;
	if (sysfs_kdamond->contexts->nr != 1)
		goto out;
	damon_sysfs_upd_tuned_intervals(sysfs_kdamond);
	damon_sysfs_upd_schemes_stats(sysfs_kdamond);
	damon_sysfs_upd_schemes_effective_quotas(sysfs_kdamond);
out:
	mutex_unlock(&damon_sysfs_lock);
	return 0;
}
```

Okay, now it's clear. `sysfs_kdamond->contexts` *is* accessed *before* any NULL check! This is definitely a potential bug! I need to trace this back to where `sysfs_kdamond` and `contexts` are initialized to confirm this hypothesis. Looks like a classic NULL pointer dereference in the making.     "thought": "**Analysis of Potential Use-After-Free Vulnerabilities in DAMON Context Management**

Okay, let's break this down. First, I'm checking the resource management in `damon_sysfs_turn_damon_on`. I'm verifying that if `damon_call` fails, the `repeat_call_control` isn't leaked, and it's not – it gets freed in `damon_call_handle_inactive_ctx`. No immediate problem there.

Now, UAFs are what I'm truly concerned about. Specifically, `damon_sysfs_repeat_call_fn` uses `sysfs_kdamond` through its `data` argument, and if that gets freed concurrently, we have a UAF. Okay, so when does `sysfs_kdamond` get freed? In `damon_sysfs_kdamond_release`, which is called when the kobject's refcount hits zero. If `damon_destroy_ctx` is called, it calls `damon_stop`.  If that hasn't finished calling `damon_sysfs_repeat_call_fn`, it could be dereferencing `sysfs_kdamond` while or after it's freed! Is the `sysfs_kdamond` freed concurrently? No, `damon_stop` is called within the destruction which means that the thread calling `damon_sysfs_repeat_call_fn` must have terminated before `damon_sysfs_kdamond_release` is called because it waits. So that eliminates that scenario.

Next, I need to understand the reported bug concerning contexts. I'm focusing on the condition where `nr_contexts` is set to 0 via sysfs while DAMON is running, potentially leading to a NULL pointer dereference. The fix added checks to ensure the contexts exist, but let's see if those are sufficient. It seems that `damon_sysfs_lock` protects access to `contexts->nr`, but not `contexts` itself.

Here's the problem: The `nr_contexts_store` function is what modifies `nr_contexts`. It calls `damon_sysfs_contexts_add_dirs`. This function calls `damon_sysfs_contexts_rm_dirs` which can free `contexts->contexts_arr` and set it to NULL.  If `nr_contexts` is set to 0, `contexts_arr` is freed. Crucially, I see that the code doesn't check if the DAMON context is running before allowing the change to zero.

Here's the critical vulnerability. If `nr_contexts_store` is called when DAMON is running, it will destroy the context while DAMON is using it! This is a classic UAF and the root cause of the bug. `nr_contexts_store` *should* check the running state of DAMON!

    "thought": "**Analyzing Potential Race Conditions in DAMON Sysfs Context Management**

Okay, so I'm looking at this `nr_contexts_store` function, and my immediate thought is, "This is dangerous." It seems we're allowing the number of contexts to be modified while DAMON is potentially running. Specifically, I see that `nr` can be 0 or 1. If it's 0, `damon_sysfs_contexts_add_dirs` is called with 0, which destroys the `contexts->contexts_arr` and sets it to NULL.  The patch addresses this with an initial check.

Now, if the DAMON daemon (`kdamond`) is running, it could be accessing these contexts through the lock, specifically in `damon_sysfs_upd_tuned_intervals`. The patch correctly skips the `contexts_arr[0]` dereference if `nr` isn't 1.  However, this doesn't protect against all scenarios.  

My primary concern is that a write to `nr_contexts` to 0 might happen while DAMON is running, which will free the context being used by the thread. After that, DAMON should check for `nr` and exit the function.

I see that `damon_sysfs_contexts_rm_dirs` deallocates everything, which could lead to use-after-free conditions because the running `kdamond` thread might still be referencing these very sysfs structures.  Specifically, `damon_sysfs_repeat_call_fn` accesses `kdamond->contexts->contexts_arr[0]`.

The patch is fixing the access to freed memory, but what if `nr` changes to 0 and back to 1 *while* the DAMON daemon is running? This is a problem, because it *could* free the context and then initialize it back!

When we set `nr` to 1, a *new* context object is allocated, and the `contexts_arr[0]` is updated with a new object.  I'm looking at `damon_sysfs_context_add_dirs`, it seems it allocates these objects.

Looking closer, `damon_sysfs_context_add_dirs` *does* initialize the attributes through `damon_sysfs_context_set_attrs` and it allocates everything.

The crucial issue is, `nr_contexts_store` does *not* check if `kdamond` is running. This is the race condition. It seems `nr_kdamonds_store` *does* check, using the `damon_sysfs_kdamonds_busy` function. `nr_contexts_store` just locks the global `damon_sysfs_lock`. I see no check for DAMON being busy in `damon_sysfs_contexts_add_dirs` either. This is the problem.

Okay, so I've been digging into this DAMON sysfs code, specifically focusing on potential concurrency issues related to the `nr_contexts_store` function. My main concern is that modifying the number of contexts while the kdamond thread is running could lead to inconsistent states, memory corruption, or even crashes.

I started by scrutinizing `nr_contexts_store` itself. It became clear that it *doesn't* directly check if kdamond is active before allowing a context count change. That's a red flag. If kdamond *is* running, a write to `nr_contexts` would destroy the existing context directories and potentially the internal DAMON data structures associated with those contexts.

My investigation led me to `damon_sysfs_upd_schemes_stats`, and specifically the access `kdamond->contexts->contexts_arr[0]`. If `nr_contexts_store` changed `nr` to 0, it would dereference a NULL pointer! The critical point to observe is that in the function `damon_sysfs_repeat_call_fn`, it now checks that `nr != 1` before calling `damon_sysfs_upd_schemes_stats` AND other similar functions.

Okay, so if the code checks if `nr != 1`, it returns `-EINVAL`. This is good. It prevents the out-of-bounds access.

But the race condition lies in writing to `nr_contexts` while the kdamond thread is running. If `nr` becomes 0 and is then changed to 1, the new `contexts_arr[0]` is a newly allocated `damon_sysfs_context`, and its `schemes` pointer is either empty or not set up. And during the `repeat_call_fn`, the access to `kdamond->contexts->contexts_arr[0]` could lead to trouble. So the patch now checks if `nr == 1` and if not it skips calling those methods.

I then went through several other functions that access the contexts. I found that functions like `damon_sysfs_commit_input` use the `damon_sysfs_lock` so no race conditions. `damon_sysfs_repeat_call_fn` also takes this same lock and does *not* block. This is clever.

I then started to check for other sysfs store functions that did *not* hold this lock. `refresh_ms_store` stood out because it doesn't take the lock, and its value is read by `damon_sysfs_repeat_call_fn`. The potential race there is on an `unsigned int`. In most modern architectures, reading and writing a `unsigned int` is atomic.

I examined `obsolete_target_store` which has a similar issue, which stores a bool. I verified that this is ok.

I checked `pid_target_store` and also verified this one is okay.

I went back and found `start_store` and `end_store`. The data race on `unsigned long` could be a problem in 32 bit architectures since this is a 64 bit type.

Finally, I confirmed that `damon_sysfs_repeat_call_fn` is safe, thanks to the use of `mutex_trylock` rather than a blocking `mutex_lock`. This prevents deadlocks.

So, in summary, the original concerns about concurrent modification of contexts led to a deep dive into how various functions interact with the context data structures. The current code *mostly* handles the race conditions correctly, particularly with the introduction of the `damon_sysfs_lock` and the guard checks for `nr != 1`. There could be race conditions on integer or bool types that may be possible, but likely benign. However, I have concluded it's unlikely this can trigger any memory issues or crashes. I was able to verify this, with the new `if (sysfs_kdamond->contexts->nr != 1)` guard that has been added to prevent out-of-bounds accesses.

The use of `damon_sysfs_lock` and `mutex_trylock` appeared mostly sound, particularly in functions directly handling user input and commands, effectively protecting against concurrent modifications to `nr`.

However, the analysis of `damon_sysfs_schemes_tried_regions_upd_one`, called from `damos_walk` within `damon_sysfs_update_schemes_tried_regions`, sparked a deeper investigation.  I initially worried about a potential race condition because `damos_walk` involves a kdamond thread that doesn't hold `damon_sysfs_lock`. But `damos_walk` blocks while the kdamond thread is running and the caller holds the `damon_sysfs_lock`! This carefully ensures that `nr` *cannot* change during the walk, and `contexts_arr[0]` remains valid. It's a clever (or fortunate) design, really.

I then expanded my search, carefully examining other sysfs `store` functions that *could* modify data used by the worker thread.  I focused on functions like `start_store` and `nr_regions_store`. This copy happens during `commit_input` and `turn_damon_on`, both of which acquire `damon_sysfs_lock`. Okay, so I'm looking at this patch and my initial thought is, "What's this doing?" It looks straightforward enough on the surface:

```c
	if (!mutex_trylock(&damon_sysfs_lock))
		return 0;
	if (sysfs_kdamond->contexts->nr != 1)
		goto out;
	damon_sysfs_upd_tuned_intervals(sysfs_kdamond);
	damon_sysfs_upd_schemes_stats(sysfs_kdamond);
	damon_sysfs_upd_schemes_effective_quotas(sysfs_kdamond);
out:
	mutex_unlock(&damon_sysfs_lock);
```

My first question is: could `sysfs_kdamond->contexts` be NULL here? I need to make sure this isn't a potential null pointer dereference. Ah, right, when `kdamonds_add_dirs` is called, `contexts` is allocated with `damon_sysfs_contexts_alloc()` and assigned. So it shouldn't be NULL. The `contexts` struct manages `nr`, which specifies the number of contexts, so it's probably safe.

Next, I need to consider locking. The code grabs the `mutex_trylock(&damon_sysfs_lock)`. Access to `sysfs_kdamond->contexts` seems to be protected, the `contexts` pointer itself should remain constant after kdamond creation, only the data inside might change. If `nr != 1`, it jumps to `out` and unlocks the mutex, so that seems okay.

Now, a bit of deeper digging. Looking at the `damon_sysfs_commit_input` function:

```c
static int damon_sysfs_commit_input(void *data)

...
	if (kdamond->contexts->nr != 1)
		return -EINVAL;
...

The comment mentions a potential null pointer dereference if `nr_contexts` is set to 0 via sysfs while DAMON is running. So, I need to figure out how `nr_contexts` (or, in the code, probably `nr`) can become 0 *while* DAMON is active.

This leads me to the real question - How can `nr_contexts` be set to 0 via sysfs *while* DAMON is running? I should check `nr_contexts_store` or similar functions, perhaps `nr_store`. I don't see it here, but maybe it is somewhere else? I need to search for it in this context. It seems like the core issue could be a race condition, where the number of contexts could be updated.

    "thought": "**Analysis of Concurrent Sysfs Access in DAMON**

Alright, let's break this down. My initial observation is that `nr_contexts_store`, which controls the number of monitoring contexts, doesn't check if the DAMON kdamond is actively running before it allows the user to modify the number of contexts. This seems like a potential race condition and a real vulnerability.

Specifically, `nr_contexts_store` calls `damon_sysfs_contexts_add_dirs`. This function frees the existing context directories and reallocates a new array (`contexts_arr`) based on the new `nr_contexts` value. If the new value is 0, everything is freed. If it is 1, a new context is created. The critical point is that this all happens while the kdamond might be running asynchronously, driven by the kdamond thread via `damon_call`. `damon_call` asks the kdamond to invoke `damon_sysfs_repeat_call_fn`.

The key here is `damon_sysfs_lock`.  The kdamond thread calls `damon_sysfs_repeat_call_fn`, which acquires `damon_sysfs_lock`, then checks `contexts->nr` and accesses `contexts_arr[0]`. The `nr_contexts_store` also acquires this lock, which means that the two are correctly serialized by the lock.  

However, it is problematic that a user can write to `nr_contexts` while `kdamond` is running. If `nr_contexts` is set to 0, `damon_sysfs_contexts_rm_dirs` will free the context, and while the kdamond thread may soon call a function accessing the now freed `contexts_arr[0]`, the lock prevents that from happening, and `damon_sysfs_repeat_call_fn` checks to see if `nr` is 0. This is good.

But, if the user writes 1, a new context will be allocated, replacing the old `contexts_arr[0]`. This will also happen while the kdamond is running. If this is the case, what is the impact?

The kdamond thread reads and writes context attributes using functions like `damon_sysfs_upd_tuned_intervals` and `damon_sysfs_upd_schemes_stats`, which access `contexts_arr[0]->attrs->intervals`. The critical observation here is that `damon_sysfs_repeat_call_fn` writes the internal state to the sysfs-facing struct.

However, the running DAMON instance, which is internally represented by `struct damon_ctx`, uses the old context, not the newly allocated one. In particular, functions that update statistics, etc. will now be writing to potentially uninitialized or incorrect values in the *new* context, because these functions call `ctx->attrs` and then write to `contexts->contexts_arr[0]->attrs`.

A bigger worry: what if a file is open under `contexts_arr[0]` when `nr_contexts` is modified? I think the refcounting on the kobject handles this, however, so the object is only freed when refs go to 0.

So, the issue is not a NULL pointer dereference (because of the lock), but rather a *data corruption*. The running DAMON instance is working with an outdated context, while the sysfs interface is presenting a *new* (and possibly incomplete) context. There is a window for the user to write 1 or 0 to `nr_contexts` while the kdamond is running, without blocking. This is a very serious bug. It can lead to inconsistencies between DAMON's internal state and the sysfs presented state, leading to erroneous DAMON operation.

First, there's a serious potential for **deadlock** in `damon_call()`.  Here's how it plays out:  `damon_call` adds a control request to a queue using `ctx->call_controls_lock`, then checks if the kdamond thread is still running with a lock-protected check `damon_is_running()`.  If the kdamond is *concurrently* exiting and about to process the queue, it'll acquire the lock, process, and then *release* the `kdamond_lock`, setting `ctx->kdamond` to `NULL`.  If `damon_call` happens to insert a request *after* the drain but *before* the NULL assignment, `damon_is_running()` will mistakenly return true.  `damon_call` then blocks on a completion variable, and the thread is gone! It's *never* signaled, leading to an infinite wait in the calling thread. The fact that `wait_for_completion` uses `TASK_UNINTERRUPTIBLE` makes this a High-severity issue, because it can create unkillable processes.

Next, I've spotted a **memory leak** in `damon_sysfs_turn_damon_on()`. The `repeat_call_control` is allocated, set up to be deallocated on cancel, and passed to `damon_call()`. If `damon_call` returns an error, the code *ignores* the error and returns.  The trouble starts if `damon_is_running()` is `false` inside `damon_call`, because the queue is processed by `damon_call_handle_inactive_ctx()` and this routine *unlinks* the request, but doesn't free it! Since  `damon_sysfs_turn_damon_on()` doesn't check the return value of `damon_call`, the memory is lost.

Finally, there's a serious **data race/starvation bug** when multiple kdamonds are running.  The global `damon_sysfs_next_update_jiffies` variable is used to schedule periodic updates. If there are multiple damons, and one is set with a *long* update interval (say, 10 seconds) and another with a short one (e.g., 100 milliseconds), the long interval will effectively starve the short one.  This is because the timers are global. Multiple instances run concurrently, writing to the same variable, with no protection!  This is a logic error that will break the behavior of some instances if it runs simultaneously with another with a different configuration.

I'm confident in these findings. I've considered other potential issues and corner cases, but these three stand out as the most critical and easy to demonstrate.

    "content": "commit 181442f7c83ab633a96d485aa2b0b649962cecaf
Author: Josh Law <objecting@objecting.org>

mm/damon/sysfs: check contexts->nr in repeat_call_fn

This commit fixes a NULL pointer dereference in damon_sysfs_repeat_call_fn().
It adds a check for contexts->nr to ensure contexts_arr[0] is not dereferenced
when nr_contexts is set to 0 via sysfs.

> diff --git a/mm/damon/sysfs.c b/mm/damon/sysfs.c
> index ddc30586c0e6..6a44a2f3d8fc 100644
> --- a/mm/damon/sysfs.c
> +++ b/mm/damon/sysfs.c
> @@ -1620,9 +1620,12 @@ static int damon_sysfs_repeat_call_fn(void *data)
>  
>  	if (!mutex_trylock(&damon_sysfs_lock))
>  		return 0;

While reviewing this function, I noticed that earlier in
damon_sysfs_repeat_call_fn(), a static global variable is used for timing:

static unsigned long damon_sysfs_next_update_jiffies;

static int damon_sysfs_repeat_call_fn(void *data)
{
        ...
        if (time_before(jiffies, damon_sysfs_next_update_jiffies))
                return 0;
        damon_sysfs_next_update_jiffies = jiffies +
                msecs_to_jiffies(sysfs_kdamond->refresh_ms);
        ...
}

Since damon_sysfs_next_update_jiffies is a static global variable, could this
cause update starvation across multiple kdamonds?

If multiple kdamond instances are running concurrently, they all read and
write this same global variable without synchronization. If Kdamond A has a
long refresh_ms and Kdamond B has a short refresh_ms, Kdamond A running first
will update the global timer to a later time.

When Kdamond B runs shortly after, time_before() would evaluate to true,
causing Kdamond B to return early without updating its stats.

> +	if (sysfs_kdamond->contexts->nr != 1)
> +		goto out;
>  	damon_sysfs_upd_tuned_intervals(sysfs_kdamond);
>  	damon_sysfs_upd_schemes_stats(sysfs_kdamond);
>  	damon_sysfs_upd_schemes_effective_quotas(sysfs_kdamond);
> +out:
>  	mutex_unlock(&damon_sysfs_lock);
>  	return 0;
>  }

Looking further into the surrounding sysfs code, could this leak the
repeat_call_control object if damon_call() fails?

In damon_sysfs_turn_damon_on(), we allocate the control and set
dealloc_on_cancel to true:

static int damon_sysfs_turn_damon_on(struct damon_sysfs_kdamond *kdamond)
{
        ...
        repeat_call_control = kmalloc_obj(*repeat_call_control);
        ...
        repeat_call_control->dealloc_on_cancel = true;
        damon_call(ctx, repeat_call_control);
        return err;
}

If the background kthread exits rapidly, damon_is_running() inside
damon_call() returns false. It then delegates to
damon_call_handle_inactive_ctx(), which deletes the control from the list and
returns -EINVAL without freeing the control. The dealloc_on_cancel logic is
only evaluated by the kthread.

Back in damon_sysfs_turn_damon_on(), the return value of damon_call() is
ignored, and the function returns the previous success err (0). The
dynamically allocated repeat_call_control is never freed.

Additionally, is it possible for damon_call() to deadlock and wait infinitely
if the background kdamond thread exits concurrently?

When damon_call() executes:

1. It acquires ctx->call_controls_lock, adds the control request, and releases
   the lock.
2. It checks damon_is_running() by acquiring kdamond_lock and verifying if
   ctx->kdamond is not NULL.

Concurrently, the background kdamond_fn() thread might be exiting. It invokes
kdamond_call(ctx, true) to drain pending controls, then acquires kdamond_lock,
sets ctx->kdamond to NULL, and exits.

If damon_call() inserts the new control into the list after kdamond_call()
has finished executing, but before ctx->kdamond is set to NULL,
damon_is_running() will still return true.

damon_call() will then proceed to wait_for_completion(&control->completion).
Because the kdamond thread has already finished processing the list and is
exiting, no one will ever complete the request, leaving the calling process
hung indefinitely in an uninterruptible sleep state.

mm/damon/sysfs: check contexts->nr in repeat_call_fn

This commit fixes a NULL pointer dereference in damon_sysfs_repeat_call_fn().
It adds a check for contexts->nr to ensure contexts_arr[0] is not dereferenced
when nr_contexts is set to 0 via sysfs.

> diff --git a/mm/damon/sysfs.c b/mm/damon/sysfs.c
> index ddc30586c0e6..6a44a2f3d8fc 100644

>  
>  	if (!mutex_trylock(&damon_sysfs_lock))
>  		return 0;

While reviewing this function, I noticed that earlier in
damon_sysfs_repeat_call_fn(), a static global variable is used for timing:

static unsigned long damon_sysfs_next_update_jiffies;

static int damon_sysfs_repeat_call_fn(void *data)
{
        ...
        if (time_before(jiffies, damon_sysfs_next_update_jiffies))
                return 0;
        damon_sysfs_next_update_jiffies = jiffies +
                msecs_to_jiffies(sysfs_kdamond->refresh_ms);
        ...
}

Since damon_sysfs_next_update_jiffies is a static global variable, could this
cause update starvation across multiple kdamonds?

If multiple kdamond instances are running concurrently, they all read and
write this same global variable without synchronization. If Kdamond A has a
long refresh_ms and Kdamond B has a short refresh_ms, Kdamond A running first
will update the global timer to a later time.

When Kdamond B runs shortly after, time_before() would evaluate to true,
causing Kdamond B to return early without updating its stats.

Looking further into the surrounding sysfs code, could this leak the
repeat_call_control object if damon_call() fails?

In damon_sysfs_turn_damon_on(), we allocate the control and set
dealloc_on_cancel to true:

static int damon_sysfs_turn_damon_on(struct damon_sysfs_kdamond *kdamond)
{
        ...
        repeat_call_control = kmalloc_obj(*repeat_call_control);
        ...
        repeat_call_control->dealloc_on_cancel = true;
        damon_call(ctx, repeat_call_control);
        return err;
}

If the background kthread exits rapidly, damon_is_running() inside
damon_call() returns false. It then delegates to
damon_call_handle_inactive_ctx(), which deletes the control from the list and
returns -EINVAL without freeing the control. The dealloc_on_cancel logic is
only evaluated by the kthread.

Back in damon_sysfs_turn_damon_on(), the return value of damon_call() is
ignored, and the function returns the previous success err (0). The
dynamically allocated repeat_call_control is never freed.

Additionally, is it possible for damon_call() to deadlock and wait infinitely
if the background kdamond thread exits concurrently?

When damon_call() executes:

1. It acquires ctx->call_controls_lock, adds the control request, and releases
   the lock.
2. It checks damon_is_running() by acquiring kdamond_lock and verifying if
   ctx->kdamond is not NULL.

Concurrently, the background kdamond_fn() thread might be exiting. It invokes
kdamond_call(ctx, true) to drain pending controls, then acquires kdamond_lock,
sets ctx->kdamond to NULL, and exits.

If damon_call() inserts the new control into the list after kdamond_call()
has finished executing, but before ctx->kdamond is set to NULL,
damon_is_running() will still return true.

damon_call() will then proceed to wait_for_completion(&control->completion).
Because the kdamond thread has already finished processing the list and is
exiting, no one will ever complete the request, leaving the calling process
hung indefinitely in an uninterruptible sleep state.
